# 5.1.3 Strategies to design new materials with high energy storage density

Beyond capacitive energy storage, the relaxor antiferroelectric (AFE) design strategy—specifically the introduction of heterogeneous dipolar structures (HDSs)—holds promise for other functional applications. The dynamic, rapidly reversible nanodomain switching observed in Cd3 ceramics (Fig. 1c) suggests potential in high-speed electromechanical actuators, where reduced hysteresis and fast response are critical. Furthermore, the temperature-dependent phase evolution from AFE to multi-cell cubic (MCC) and paraelectric (PE_c) phases (Fig. 4c,d) indicates that these materials could serve as tunable dielectric elements in thermal sensors or pyroelectric energy harvesters, leveraging the broadened free energy minima (Fig. 4e) to achieve stable performance over a wide temperature range. The ability to engineer local compositional disorder to disrupt long-range ordering, as demonstrated by the progressive reduction in domain size from PH to Cd3 (Fig. 1a–c), also provides a pathway for designing low-loss, high-permittivity dielectrics for microwave communication devices. However, the text_claim_check for Fig. 1 notes that the mechanistic link between disordered modulation and bound charge redistribution is "exaggerated," as it is not directly visualized. This underscores a critical gap: while the phenomenological benefits of HDSs are clear, the underlying charge dynamics and local field rearrangement remain inferred rather than proven. Future work should employ in-situ spectroscopic or scanning probe techniques to directly correlate the proposed charge redistribution with the observed polarization behavior, thereby validating the universality of this design principle across different application domains.
